section.title: Love One Another
section.sectionRationale: John presses the message heard from the beginning: love one another. He sets Cain, who hated and murdered, against Jesus, who laid down His life, and shows that real love acts sacrificially. Such love assures our hearts before God, and John ties it to the twofold command to believe in the Son and love one another, sealed by the Spirit.
section.thesis: Love one another is the message from the beginning, and it is defined not by Cain's murderous hatred but by Jesus laying down His life, so that love shown in costly action assures our hearts before God, who commands us to believe in His Son and love one another, and who abides in us by His Spirit.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the message to love, with Cain as the anti-pattern; love defined by the cross and shown in action; hearts assured before God; and the command to believe and love, sealed by the Spirit.

verses.text: This is the message you have heard from the beginning: We should {{agapao|love}} one another.
Do not be like Cain, who belonged to the evil one and murdered his brother. And why did Cain slay him? Because his own deeds were evil, while those of his brother were righteous.
So do not be surprised, brothers, if the world hates you.
We know that we have passed from death to life, because we love our brothers. The one who does not love remains in death.
Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer, and you know that eternal life does not reside in a murderer.
By this we know what love is: Jesus {{tithemi|laid down}} His life for us, and we ought to lay down our lives for our brothers.
If anyone with earthly possessions sees his brother in need, but withholds his compassion from him, how can the {{agape|love}} of God abide in him?
Little children, let us love not in word and speech, but in action and truth.

units.label: Hearts assured before God
units.note: God is greater than our hearts

verses.text: And by this we will know that we belong to the truth, and will assure our hearts in His presence:
Even if our hearts condemn us, God is greater than our hearts, and He knows all things.
Beloved, if our hearts do not condemn us, we have {{parrhesia|confidence}} before God,
and we will receive from Him whatever we ask, because we keep His commandments and do what is pleasing in His sight.
And this is His {{entole|commandment}}: that we should believe in the name of His Son, Jesus Christ, and we should love one another just as He commanded us.
Whoever keeps His commandments remains in God, and God in him. And by this we know that He remains in us: by the {{pneuma|Spirit}} He has given us.

agapao.gloss: To love, to seek another's good with self-giving devotion. The command to love one another is the message heard from the beginning. It is not mere sentiment but active goodwill that shows itself in deeds, the very opposite of Cain's hatred.

tithemi.gloss: To lay down, place, set. Jesus laid down His life for us, freely giving Himself to death. The same verb appears in John's Gospel where Jesus lays down His life for the sheep. It defines love at its deepest and sets the pattern His people follow toward one another.

agape.gloss: Love, self-giving love that seeks the good of another. The love of God, whether His love in us or love that comes from Him, cannot truly abide in someone who closes his heart to a brother in need. Real agape opens the hand as well as the mouth.

parrhesia.gloss: Confidence, boldness, freedom of speech. When our hearts do not condemn us, we have boldness before God, especially in prayer. It is the freedom of a child before a loving Father, not the arrogance of the self-assured, and it flows from belonging to the truth.

entole.gloss: Commandment, charge, instruction. John sums up God's commandment as two joined things: believe in the name of His Son and love one another. Faith in Christ and love for others are not rivals but a single command, the shape of the life that abides in God.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. The Holy Spirit whom God has given is the inner witness that God abides in us. The Spirit both assures believers of their union with God and empowers the obedient, loving life that gives outward evidence of it.

basic.context: The message from the very beginning is simple and hard: love one another. John holds up two opposite examples. Cain hated his righteous brother and murdered him; Jesus loved us and laid down His life. Real love is not just words but action, like sharing with a brother in need. This kind of love assures our hearts that we belong to God, and even when our conscience accuses us, God is greater than our hearts. He commands us to believe in Jesus and love each other, and He gives His Spirit as the seal that He lives in us.
basic.hermeneutics: John continues the test of true faith, now focused on love shown in deeds. He is not making love the payment for salvation but the evidence of it: we know we have passed from death to life because we love. The famous words of verses 19 and 20 comfort troubled believers, God is greater than our accusing hearts, while the whole passage still warns that a loveless life is a sign of death, not life.
basic.originalLanguages: The message is to love, agapao, one another. Love is defined by the cross: Jesus laid down, tithemi, His life for us. The love, agape, of God cannot abide in one who shuts his heart to a brother in need. When our hearts do not condemn us we have confidence, parrhesia, before God. His commandment, entole, is to believe and love, and He abides in us by the Spirit, pneuma, He has given.
basic.typology: Cain and Abel, the first brothers, become the pattern of the world's hatred for the righteous, fulfilled supremely when the world hated and killed Jesus, the truly righteous One. Jesus laying down His life fulfils the sacrificial love foreshadowed in the offerings and defines love for all who follow Him.
basic.christ: Jesus is the definition of love: He laid down His life for us, and His self-giving is both the ground of our new life and the pattern for ours. He is the Son in whose name we are commanded to believe, and He commands us to love one another as He has loved us.
basic.god: God is the one who commands love and defines it by His Son's sacrifice. He is greater than our accusing hearts, knowing all things, and He gives His Spirit as the inner assurance that He abides in us. He is both the judge before whom we stand and the Father who gives us confidence.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 4 records Cain and Abel. John 15:13 says greater love has no one than to lay down his life. John 13:34 gives the command to love as Jesus loved, and Romans 8 speaks of the Spirit witnessing that we are God's children.
basic.oneStory: From the first brothers the world has been divided between hatred and love, Cain and Abel. The whole story turns on the righteous One, Jesus, who was hated and yet laid down His life in love. Now His people, born of God and sealed by the Spirit, are called to that same self-giving love, the mark of those who have passed from death to life.

context.literary: The section restates the message to love, then develops it through contrast, Cain against Jesus, and definition, love is laying down your life. It moves to the inward effect, assurance of heart before God, and closes with the twofold command and the witness of the Spirit, which leads into the next section on testing the spirits.
context.historical: The secessionist teachers who had left the fellowship evidently failed the test of love toward the believers they abandoned. John counters their claims by making sacrificial love, expressed even in sharing possessions, the visible mark of those who have truly passed from death to life.
context.canonical: The passage draws on Genesis 4, on Jesus' teaching that laying down one's life is the greatest love, John 15:13, and on His new command to love, John 13:34. The inner witness of the Spirit joins Romans 8:16, and the union language of abiding echoes John 15.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press the command to love one another as the mark of those who have passed from death to life, to define that love by Christ's self-sacrifice and show it in concrete action, and to assure believers before God while binding faith in the Son and love for one another into a single command sealed by the Spirit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage prescribes love, let us love in action and truth, and describes its meaning, love is that Jesus laid down His life, and its effect, assurance of heart. The warning that a loveless person remains in death describes a spiritual reality, not a threat to those who genuinely love.

debates.question: What does it mean that 'God is greater than our hearts' when our hearts condemn us (verses 19-20)?

views.label: Comfort: God's verdict of grace outweighs a self-accusing conscience
views.case: For believers whose lives show real, if imperfect, love, an over-scrupulous conscience may still accuse them; John comforts them that God, who knows all things, sees the genuine love they cannot always feel, and His gracious verdict is greater than their fearful hearts. This fits the pastoral aim of assurance running through the letter and the immediate context of loving in action and truth. It is the strongest and most common reading.

views.label: Warning: God's greater knowledge sees more than our hearts admit
views.case: Some read it that if even our own heart condemns us, how much more does God, who knows everything, so it is a sobering call to self-examination rather than comfort.

views.label: A deliberate two-edged statement
views.case: Others hold John means both: God is greater as comfort for the humble and as warning to the complacent, depending on the state of the heart.

debates.bearing: In context, aimed at believers who love in deed and truth and long for assurance, the strongest reading is comfort: God's gracious knowledge and verdict are greater than an accusing conscience. Yet the same truth soberly reminds the complacent that God sees all, so genuine love, not mere feeling, is what assures the heart before Him.

study.originalLanguages: The message heard from the beginning is to agapao, to love one another, a love that acts, not merely feels. John defines it by the cross: Jesus tithemi, laid down, placed down His very life for us, the same verb used of the good shepherd in John's Gospel. So the agape, the love, of God cannot be said to abide in anyone who sees a brother in need and shuts his heart. Where such love is real, our hearts are assured, and we come with parrhesia, boldness and freedom, before God. His entole, His commandment, joins two things into one: believe in the name of the Son and love one another. And the proof that God abides in us is the pneuma, the Spirit, He has given.

typology.type: Cain who hated and murdered his righteous brother
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of the world's hatred for the righteous, culminating in the world's hatred of Jesus, the truly righteous One, John 15:18-25.

typology.type: Jesus laying down His life for us
typology.fulfillment: The sacrificial love foreshadowed in the offerings, now revealed as the very definition of love and the pattern for His people, John 15:13.

study.christ: Christ is love defined: Jesus laid down His life for us, and that self-giving is both the ground of our passage from death to life and the model for our love toward one another. He is the righteous brother whom the world hates, as it hated Abel, and the Son in whose name we are commanded to believe. To follow Him is to lay down our lives, in whole or in the daily giving of our possessions and compassion, for the brothers and sisters He has given us.
study.god: God commands love and shows what it is in the sacrifice of His Son. He is the searcher of hearts, greater than our self-accusing consciences, who knows all things and whose gracious verdict steadies the troubled believer. He binds faith and love into one command and gives His own Spirit as the inner witness that He abides in us, so that assurance rests finally on His work, not on the fluctuations of our feelings.

crossReferences.note: Cain murders his righteous brother Abel, the anti-pattern of love John holds up as a warning.
Greater love has no one than this, that he lay down his life for his friends, the love Jesus embodies and defines.
Love one another as I have loved you; by this all will know you are My disciples, the mark John presses.
The Spirit Himself testifies with our spirit that we are children of God, matching the Spirit's inner witness John names.

study.oneStory: Since Cain and Abel, humanity has been split between hatred and love, and the world has hated the righteous. The story turns on the truly righteous One, Jesus, hated by the world yet laying down His life in love. Now those born of God and sealed by His Spirit are called into that same self-giving love, loving not in word only but in action and truth. Such love is the sure sign of having passed from death to life, the new-creation family the whole story has been forming.

sermon.title: Loving One Another
sermon.why: Begg preaches this passage, 1 John 3:11-18, on the message to love, Cain versus the love of Christ, and love shown in action and truth. A clear, orthodox handling of love as the mark of those who have passed from death to life.

quiz.q: Why does John hold up Cain as a warning in verse 12?

options.text: Because Cain hated and murdered his righteous brother, the opposite of the love we are called to.
options.note: Cain is the anti-pattern: hatred of a righteous brother, springing from evil deeds.

options.text: Because Cain was punished more harshly than he deserved.
options.note: John's point is Cain's hatred, not the justice of his punishment.

options.text: Because Cain simply made a careless mistake.
options.note: It was deliberate hatred and murder, not a mere mistake.

options.text: Because Cain worked hard but was overlooked.
options.note: The issue is that his deeds were evil and he hated his righteous brother, not that he was overlooked.

quiz.q: How does John define what love is in verse 16?

options.text: Jesus laid down His life for us, and we ought to lay down our lives for our brothers.
options.note: Love is defined by Christ's self-sacrifice and patterned for His people.

options.text: Love is mainly a warm feeling toward others.
options.note: John defines love by sacrificial action, not feeling alone.

options.text: Love is saying kind words when convenient.
options.note: John insists on love in action and truth, not word and speech only.

options.text: Love is admiring good people from a distance.
options.note: Real love gives itself for others, as Jesus did, not merely admires them.

quiz.q: What does verse 17 say about love and a brother in need?

options.text: Shutting your heart to a brother in need shows the love of God does not abide in you.
options.note: Real love opens the hand; withholding compassion contradicts the love of God.

options.text: Helping the needy is optional for spiritual people.
options.note: John makes practical compassion a test of genuine love, not an option.

options.text: Only feelings of pity matter, not action.
options.note: John calls for action and truth, not feelings that never move to help.

options.text: Generosity earns your salvation.
options.note: Generosity is evidence of God's love abiding, not the purchase of salvation.

quiz.q: What does John mean that 'God is greater than our hearts' when our hearts condemn us?

options.text: For those who love in deed and truth, God's gracious verdict outweighs an accusing conscience.
options.note: In context it comforts believers whose genuine love may not still every fear.

options.text: God overlooks sin so we need never examine ourselves.
options.note: John still calls for real love; it is not license to ignore sin.

options.text: Our feelings are the final measure of our standing with God.
options.note: The point is the opposite; God, not our fluctuating hearts, is the measure.

options.text: God's greater knowledge only ever condemns us further.
options.note: That is one sobering edge, but the primary aim here is assurance for those who love genuinely.

quiz.q: What is God's commandment, according to verse 23?

options.text: To believe in the name of His Son, Jesus Christ, and to love one another.
options.note: John joins faith in the Son and love for one another into a single command.

options.text: To believe correct doctrine without loving anyone.
options.note: Faith and love are bound together; belief without love is incomplete.

options.text: To love others while it does not matter what you believe.
options.note: Love matters, but so does believing in the name of the Son; both are commanded.

options.text: To keep a long list of rituals to earn favour.
options.note: The command is faith in Christ and love, not ritual to earn favour.

questions: The message from the beginning is to love one another. Who is the brother or sister God is calling you to love in action, not just in words, this week?
Love is defined by Jesus laying down His life. What would laying down your life, your time, comfort, or resources, look like for someone in your church or family?
John ties love to sharing with a brother in material need. Is there a need you have seen but passed by, and how might you respond with real compassion?
When your heart condemns you, God is greater than your heart. How does it help to know that God's gracious knowledge of you outweighs your accusing conscience?
God's command joins believing in Jesus and loving one another. Do you tend to emphasise one of these over the other, and how can you hold them together?

translationNotes.note: 'Laid down His life' uses the verb tithemi, the same expression John's Gospel uses for the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep, John 10:11.
The Greek is compressed and can be punctuated in more than one way; the sense is that genuine love assures the heart, and that God, who knows all, is greater than a self-condemning conscience.
'His commandment' is singular yet contains two parts, believing in the Son and loving one another, showing that John treats faith and love as one inseparable charge.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 3:11-24. No em dashes in this passage.
